posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I wanted to draw the attention of any Francophone Berserkers, or anyone who can use google translate, to this excellent article by Michel Bettane, which offers a synoptic view of agronomic, winemaking and cultural changes in Burgundy over the last 50 years. Lots of great analysis and insight! And masterful writing, too.

mybettanedesseauve.fr – 15 Jul 24

La Bourgogne, l’impasse et l’issue

L’œil aigu, porté par quarante vendanges de suite, est le témoin indispensable d’une histoire déroutante. Et il répond à deux bonnes questions : « Comment en est-on arrivé là ? », « Comment sortir du piège ? ». Une monographie explosive et exclusive

link_counts.title: La Bourgogne, l’impasse et l’issue | En Magnum

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Terrific article. The anecdote about Delmas realizing a few freaky vines of Merlot with smoky tasting skins at Haut Brion was illuminating.

For those whose French is stuck at the 10th grade level, Google is our friend!

www-mybettanedesseauve-fr.translate.goog – 15 Jul 24

La Bourgogne, l’impasse et l’issue

L’œil aigu, porté par quarante vendanges de suite, est le témoin indispensable d’une histoire déroutante. Et il répond à deux bonnes questions : « Comment en est-on arrivé là ? », « Comment sortir du piège ? ». Une monographie explosive et exclusive

Question for @William_Kelley - from a volume standpoint, where is Burgundy overall on its conversion to the new understanding of the roles that vine genetics, soil health, etc play in terms of quality of wine? We tend to focus on standout and vanguard producers here on WB, but is it that 20% of Burgundy has started to apply this new understanding? 10%? Obviously this is something that will take decades to change fully, as massale selection doesn’t happen over night (or without capital). But it gives one hope that there could be many more outstanding producers in Burgundy in the future if the work continues.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Really hard to put numbers to many of these things, unfortunately. Of the Côte d’Or’s vineyards taken as a whole, 31% were farmed organically as of 2022. But that is just one metric, and there are very good producers who are not certified organic (as they might use phosphonates in order to reduce doses of copper, or simply not want to pay for certification and go through the audit process), just as there are some pretty bad farmers who are nonetheless certified organic.

I would say that overall, the Burgundians ended up being pretty forward-looking with regards to vine genetics when compared with many other wine regions (and despite incentives: there is no government subsidy, for example, for replanting if you don’t use clones), and that a lot of great Pinot genetics have been preserved.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: It was interesting when I was in Burgundy last summer. Some producers were training the vines higher, etc., as William has discussed in previous posts. One place I visited did it only on a trial plot of villages level wines waiting to see how if they like the results there before adopting it more widely and another really excellent producer I visited seemed to be intrigued by it but had not adopted it year, at least as of last year.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Great article! Sometimes reading the descriptions of Burgundy in the 70s and 80s you wonder how great vintages like 1985 could ever have happened!

His discussion of “climatomania” and the inadequacy of vineyard designations and ranks for characterizing soils and wine quality was very illuminating.

posts.name: Eric Tomasi
posts.cooked:
So, I had to use google translate. Can someone who can read french tell me if the writer came off as really insufferable smug in places? or whether that is an artifact of the translation?

I would never suggest the return to the old practices, but its ts interesting how so many of the greatest burgs I’ve had came the the timeframe he is denigrating, such as the the amazing '78 vintage or all the wonderful '60s burgs i’ve had. Its probably just cause I like old wine, lol. The closest post-90 burgs I’ve had at that level that with were a '91 and '93 Ponsot.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked:
Bettane has never been known for self-deprecation, but even when he seems wrong he is always interesting- as an example his assessments thirty years ago of the virtues of Dominique Laurent and the vices of Comte de Vogué seemed weird at the time but have been validated by time. His enthusiasm for 2003 as a red burgundy vintage still seems very wide of the mark to me, but I’m sure his sample is a thousand times larger than mine.
Does anyone know what happened to Guy Accad-and has anyone witnessed the greatness he predicted for the wines of Pernin-Rossin?
On terroir, I have long maintained that conventional notions of the characters of particular villages were built on the recipes of negociants who simply provided wines to fit those preconceptions. To this day one witnesses all sorts of nonsense about , for example. the delicacy of Chambolle and Volnay and the beefiness of Nuits and Pommard even though it has become clear that both characterisations are absurd.

posts.name: Daniel Schoeff
posts.cooked:
My rather limited experience of only a few hundred burgundies over the last decade always made me think that maybe it was just me that didn’t get it…

Many if not most of the most experienced burgundy drinkers on this board will most certainly refer to wines that are “very much [insert village]” in character. Or the alternative counter “not very…”

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I don’t think the tone comes over that way in French.

The trends regarding agrochemicals, clones and yields really started to bite in the 1970s, but they took time to do their work, and of course some producers bucked the trend on some or all counts, for varying amounts of time. Clones, for example, came in in 1971, so you didn’t see clonal vineyards producing wine until later in the decade…

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: Accad is apparently still consulting. Etienne Grivot still uses him for earth analysis.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: A very well-written and insightful article with many important points. What I found most interesting was his take on take on the lack of scientific support for the Burgundy producers and the non-French contribution when science finally arrived. I think the weave he alluded to of science combined with spirited empirical work done by the growers themselves is an important insight into the quality revolution of the last 40 something years. In innovation studies the point is often made that science do not necessary preceed invention and innovation, but is there to explain empirical novelty. Burgundy viticulture and vinification may be an example of that.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: Grivot said this in Nov. 22. Time must have stood still, or he wanted to honour the man.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Maybe they didn’t do any analysis for a few years?
